payload.input: The doorbell chimes at eleven-fifty-eight, three notes that descend like a sigh. Maya rises from the couch, her thighs peeling apart with a soft sound of damp skin separating from cotton. Through the peephole, she sees a woman who must be Vivian—tall, wrapped in a camel-colored coat despite the heat, her hair not pulled back in a severe chignon but falling in loose, dark waves that catch the morning light. She is beautiful in a way that makes Maya’s stomach clench, her mouth full and painted a deep burgundy, her eyes lined with something smudged that suggests she slept in her makeup or cried it back on deliberately. Beside her stands Julian, the camera bag slung across his chest not like military gear but like an accessory, his beard not merely trimmed but sculpted, his sunglasses pushed up into his hair to reveal eyes that are heavy-lidded and immediately, unmistakably, focused on Maya’s body through the door. He is hard. Maya can see the shape of him straining against the fine wool of his trousers, and he does not try to hide it. He leans against the doorframe with the casual slouch of a man who knows he is being looked at, one hand in his pocket, the thumb of the other tracing slow circles across his lower lip as he waits. Ethan appears beside her, and Maya turns to look at him—really look—and feels a jolt of heat low in her belly. He has showered. His hair, usually a sandy mess, is swept back with the expensive pomade their aunt sent last Christmas, the scent of sandalwood and tobacco rising from his scalp in waves that make her want to bury her face in his neck. He wears the white button-down that had been hanging in the dry-cleaning bag, the fabric pulling slightly across his shoulders, his khakis pressed, his feet bare and vulnerable against the hardwood. He looks edible. He looks like bait. "They’re early," Ethan whispers, his voice cracking on the second syllable, his eyes fixed on the peephole where Julian’s thumb still traces his mouth.
